translations.source_text: 使死者無知，則已矣，若其有知，吾何面目以見員也．
translations.translation:
Pang Yu (Ziyi)
龐淯 (子異)
Lived ADca.168–ca.225
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 18 (Wei 18)
byZL181
onMarch 31st, 2025

Pang Yu, styledZiyi, was fromBiaoshi[表氏],Jiuquan[酒泉].
He defeated theChiefof the Qiang barbarians as aClerkofLiang province[涼州從事]. It happened that theGrand AdministratorofWuwei[武威太守],Zhang Meng, rebelled, and killed theInspector,Handan Shang.[Zhang] Mengordered, “Whoever dares wail at the funeral of[Handan] Shangshall be killed without pardon.” When[Pang] Yuheard of this, he abandoned his office, rushed day and night, then wailed at where the funeral was. When he was finished, he went to the gates of[Zhang] Meng. He wanted to take advantage of the meeting to kill[Zhang] Meng, for he had a dagger concealed in his clothes.[Zhang] Mengknew he was a righteous gentleman, so he ordered that he was not to be killed. From this, his loyalty and zeal became known.AGrand AdministratorXu Yiinvited him to serve asMaster of Records. Later, a person of the commandery,Huang Ang, rebelled and sieged the city.[Pang] Yuabandoned his wife and children to scale the city walls overnight and leave the siege. He urgently reported to the two commanderies ofZhangye[張掖] andDunhuang[燉煌]. They initially doubted him and were unwilling to send troops, but when[Pang] Yuintended to slay himself with a blade, the two commanderies were moved by his righteousness and thus mustered troops. The army had not arrived when the commandery city fell and[Xu] Yidied.[Pang] Yuthus collected [the corpse of][Xu] Yifor mourning and sent him back to his home commandery. He wore [mourning] clothes for three years before reverting back. When theGrand Progenitorheard this, he recruited him to be abureaucratic clerk.
TheSummary of Weistates: The troops of[Zhang] Mengwanted to go and bind[Pang] Yu, but when[Zhang] Mengheard about this, he sighed and said, “I am guilty of killing anInspector. That man is famous for his loyalty being so extreme, and were I also to kill him, how would that encourage the righteous gentlemen of this province?” And so he let him wear [mourning] clothes.
TheSummarized Documentsstates:Zhang Meng, styledShuwei, was fromDunhuang[燉煌]. The father of[Zhang] Mengwas [Zhang] Huan [張奐], who during the reign of theConquering Emperorserved successively asCommandery Administrator,Household General, andMinister of Ceremonies, then resided inHuayin[華陰] and buried there when he passed. During the beginning of theera,[Zhang] Mengserved his commandery asOfficer of Merit. At the time, the four commanderies of theHexi region1[河西] were far from the government seat ofLiang province[涼州] and were blocked by theYellow River[河] bandits, so they petitioned with a request to establish a province. An edict hadHandan ShangofChenliu[陳留] becomeInspectorofYong province[雍州刺史], with him overseeing the four commanderies. At the time, the title ofGrand AdministratorofWuwei[武威太守] was vacant. An edict, on the account that the father of[Zhang] Menghad an authoritative reputation in theHexi region[河西], had[Zhang] Mengfill it.[Handan] Shangand[Zhang] Mengboth went west. Previously,[Zhang] Mengand[Handan] Shangwere of the same age, and they mocked and insulted each other, and when they went together to their offices, they complained about each other even more while on the road. As they arrived,[Handan] Shangintended to execute[Zhang] Meng, but[Zhang] Mengknew about this and thus led troops to attack[Handan] Shang. The government residence[Handan] Shangwas in was near [that of][Zhang] Meng, and when[Handan] Shangheard troops arriving, he was fearful and ascended the house. He called to[Zhang] Mengby his style name, saying, “Shuwei, do you intend to kill me? If knowledge of my death goes out, your clan will be exterminated. I ask to reconcile! Is that still possible?”[Zhang] Mengthus called, “Come!”[Handan] Shangthus leapt from the house to[Zhang] Meng.[Zhang] Mengthen rebuked him several times and when their conversation ended, he had[Handan] Shangdelivered to theDistrict Inspector. TheDistrict Inspectorregistered[Handan] Shangto be confined to a post station.2Later,[Handan] Shangwanted to flee, but this plan was leaked and so they killed him. That year was the fourteenth year of theera [209].
When the fifteen year came [210], generalHan Suipetitioned that he would suppress[Zhang] Meng.[Zhang] Mengsent troops east to block him, but the officials and peasants feared[Han] Suiand they instead all attacked[Zhang] Meng. In the past, when [Zhang] Huan served asGrand AdministratorofWuwei[武威太守],[Zhang] Mengwas still in the womb. His mother dreamt that she carried the seal and ribbon of [Zhang] Huan, and that she ascended a tower and sung. She told [Zhang] Huan in the morning. [Zhang] Huan asked a dream diviner, and they said, “The lady will give birth to a son, and if he governs this very commandery later, he will surely die in office!” And when[Zhang] Mengwas being attacked, he knew he would die. He said, “If the dead have no cognizance, then that is it. If they have cognizance, how can I pass throughHuayin[華陰] and the grave of my father when going eastward?3” He ascended a tower, burnt it, and thusdied.•••
The four commanderies are presumablyWuwei,Jiuquan,Dunhuang, andZhangye.↩
Besides inspecting local officials for lawful behavior, theDistrict Inspectorwas also in charge of the post stations.↩
This is probably inspired by a quote by Fuchai, King of Wu. It is said that when he was killed, he said, “If the dead have no cognizance, then that is it. If they have cognizance, then how can I faceWu Yun?” [使死者無知，則已矣，若其有知，吾何面目以見員也．]↩
After theCivil Emperortreaded the throne steps, he was conferred to beCommandant of Escorting Cavalry, then promoted toGrand AdministratorofXihai[西海太守] and conferred peerage ofMarquis within the Passes. Later, he was summoned to becomePalace Attendant Grandee. When he died, his son,[Pang] Zeng, succeeded him.
***
Previously, the maternal grandfather of[Pang] Yu, Zhao An [趙安], was killed by Li Shou [李壽] of the same county, and the three maternal uncles of[Pang] Yuall fell ill and died at the time, which was fortunate for the family of [Li] Shou. The mother of[Pang] Yu,[Zhao] E, was pained that the blood feud of her father had not been repaid, so she tucked a blade in the curtains of a chariot, then in sunlight, stabbed [Li] Shou before the capital district, and when she was done, she calmly went to the county. Her expression never wavered, and she said, “The blood feud of my father has been repaid. I ask to be slain.” TheChiefofFulu[祿福長], Yin Jia [尹嘉], untied his seal and ribbon to release[Zhao] E, but she was unwilling to leave, so she had to be carried back home through force. It happened there was a pardon, so she was absolved. The province and commandery were in admiration, and they carved stone [of her deed] to mark the gates of hervillage.B
TheBiographies of Exemplary WomenbyHuangfu Mistates: The fierce woman ofWuwei[酒泉],Pang Eqin, was the wife of Pang Zixia [龐子夏] ofBiaoshi[表氏] and the daughter of Mister Zhao An [趙君安] ofFulu[祿福]. Mister [Zhao] An was killed by Li Shou [李壽] of the same county.[Pang] Eqinhad three younger brothers, all of whom wanted to avenge the feud. [Li] Shou made great defenses against them. It happened that there was a plague and all the three men died. When [Li] Shou heard this, he was very delighted. He assembled his clansmen and kin, and celebrated together with them. He said, “The strong men of the Zhao clan are all dead! The only one left is aweak daughter; how can she become a cause for concern?” He let down his guard. The son of[Pang] Eqin,[Pang] Yu, went outside to wander about when he heard those words from [Li] Shou. He returned and informed[Pang] Eqinof them.[Pang] Eqinhad always wanted to repay the blood feud, and when she heard of what [Li] Shou said, her feelings became more intense, sorrowfully shedding tears and saying, “Li Shou, you shall not enjoy happiness! Even to the end, I shall never let you live! To live under Heaven and above Earth for my household was disgraced by my three brothers! How can you know I cannot wield a blade and kill you? Think you may be lucky?” She secretly bought renowned swords, clasping long ones under her arms while holding short ones. Day and night, she felt sorrowful, and her will was in killing [Li] Shou. [Li] Shou as a person was violent and haughty. When he heard the words of[Pang] Eqin, he promptly rode a horse with a blade on his belt. The villagers were all in fear of him. A neighbor, a lady of the Xu clan [徐氏], feared that[Pang] Eqincould not curb him and feared that she would be harmed instead, so she always protested and checked her, saying, “Li Shou is a man. He has always been violent and wicked, and now he has put up safeguards around himself. Although you have a ferocious and fierce temperament, the strong and the weak cannot compete. Should you fail to curb him, then disaster from [Li] Shou will befall you and your household would be destroyed, and the pain and shame would be great. I hope that you will be careful in your actions and plan for your household.”[Pang] Eqinsaid, “The enemy of my father and mother cannot share Heaven and Earth nor experience the months and days with me! If Li Shou does not die, I would only be watching and breathing in this world! What else would I seek in life? Even though my three little brothers have died young and my household has been eradicated, I am still here. How could I borrow the hands of others? If you use your heart to liken mine to it, then Li Shou cannot be killed, but if you look at my heart, then [Li] Shou will certainly be slain by me, and that is clear!” For many nights, she grinded the sword she wielded on whetstone, and upon finishing, she would wring her hands and gnash her teeth, crying tears of sadness and heaving out long sighs. Her family and neighbors all laughed at her.[Pang] Eqintold those around her, “You all laugh at me, thinking I, a weak daughter, cannot kill [Li] Shou. But I will have the blood from his neck taint this blade! Just you all watch!” She abandoned family affairs and rode in a carriage drawn by deer to spy on [Li] Shou. In the first ten days of the second month of the second year of theera [179], in sunlight and tranquil air, she was before the capital district when she chanced upon [Li] Shou. She dismounted her carriage and grabbed the horse of [Li] Shou, scolding him. [Li] Shou was frightened and turned his horse around, intending to flee.[Pang] Eqinswung with might her blade and sliced into him, also harming the horse. The horse was terrified and [Li] Shou was pushed into a ditch next to the road.[Pang] Eqinsearched for him and went to the area, then sliced into him again. The swing hit a tree and it broke the sword she wielded. [Li] Shou was wounded, but he was not yet dead.[Pang] Eqinapproached, wanting to take the sword [Li] Shou had at his side to kill [Li] Shou. [Li] Shou shielded his sword with eyes of hate, shouting loudly, and jumping around as he got up.[Pang] Eqinpromptly straightened her body and raised her hands, her left pushing against his forehead and her right gripping his throat. She then repeatedly spun him around, who then fell. She pulled out his blade and severed the head of [Li] Shou, then held it while going to the capital district and admitted to the crime to the relevant officials. She slowly walked to the prison, her tone and expression not changing. At the time, theChiefofFulu[祿福長], Yin Jia [尹嘉] ofHanyang[漢陽], could not bear to judge[Pang] Eqin, so he untied his seal and ribbon and left his office, loosening the law to free her.[Pang] Eqinsaid, “To repay a feud with the killing of one was my manifest responsibility; to judge a case and give out punishment is your fixed obligation. Dare I be so greedy for life that you must abandon your official duties?” When the villagers heard of this, they overturned the city4in rushing to her, the observers being like a wall. There were none who did not feel sadness, joy, passion, and admiration for her. TheCommandery Commandantdid not free her in public, but secretly told her to leave and to hide herself at the earliest convenience. With a loud voice,[Pang] Eqinspoke, “To wrong the law and escape from death are not my intentions. Now that my nemesis has been wiped out, death is my responsibility. I plead for the law to take care of me so that the national system may be intact. Even if I should die ten thousand more times, I am utterly content. I would not dare to be so greedy for life that I would burden the wise administration.” Because theCommandery Commandantdid not listen and seize her,[Pang] Eqinspoke again, saying, “Even though I, a foolish woman, is trivial, I still know the rules. The law does not permit liberty in the crime of killing a person. Now that I have violated it, it would be unjust to flee. I plead to receive punishment and execution! Let my body be displayed in the morning market to revere and display the royal rules! This is my wish!” Her speech was becoming more sharp, her face having no trace of fear. TheCommandery Commandantknew that she would be hard to change, so he forcibly transported her back to her household. TheInspectorofLiang province[涼州刺史], Zhou Hong [周洪], and theGrand AdministratorofJiuquan[酒泉太守], Liu Ban [劉班], among others, petitioned together, praising her ferociousness and righteousness. They carved stone and erected a stele, displaying them at her village gates. TheMinister of Ceremonies, Zhang Huan [張奐] ofHongnong[弘農], admired and valued her conduct, and had twenty bundles of silk gifted to her. Among those within the Seas that heard about her, there were none that did not change their expression into one of praise nor regard her justice asnoble.•••
The phrase “overturning the city” [傾城] is often employed in literature to describe the extreme beauty of a woman. This is not the case here.↩
The formerAttending Gentleman at the Yellow Gates,Liang KuanofAnding[安定], reminisced on[Pang] Eqin, and thus wrote a biography for her. TheMaster of Esoteric Reposebelieved that when there was a nemesis of father and mother where Heaven and Earth could not be shared with them, a male was to do the deed. And yet,[Pang] Eqin, despite being trivial as a weak female, had retained the sorrow over the disgrace of her father and was galvanized by the wicked words of her nemeses. She swung with might her blade into the neck of her nemesis, with both man and horse being destroyed, thus putting an end to the spiritual resentment of her deceased father and wiping away the eternal regret of her three younger brothers. Never was there such a deed since late antiquity. TheBook of Odesstated, “I shall mend my lance and spear, and we shall deal with our foe together.” This can be said about[Pang] Eqin.
